food_subgroup: Beef dishes — teppanyaki / hotplate style with vegetables and sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for teppanyaki hotplate beef set; FOODON_00002764 (beef dish) is the nearest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (beef meat dish) is the closest parent; no specific teppanyaki beef set code exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for teppanyaki hotplate beef set as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One hotplate beef set typically comprises approximately 120–150 g of sliced beef, 80–100 g of mixed vegetables, and 150–160 g of steamed white rice, totalling roughly 400 g per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Beef, stir-fried with vegetables and sauce — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked beef (NDB 13365), mixed stir-fried vegetables, soy-based sauce, and steamed white rice
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for teppanyaki hotplate beef set exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data: cooked lean beef (~120 g per 400 g set, ~26 g protein/100 g, ~10 g fat/100 g), mixed stir-fried vegetables (~80 g), soy-based teriyaki sauce (~30 g), and steamed white rice (~160 g, ~2.7 g protein/100 g, ~28 g carbohydrate/100 g). Composite per-100 g values calculated by weighted average across the full 400 g set. Sodium elevated due to soy-based sauce contribution.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice (boiled) GI ~64–72; mixed beef and vegetable dish with rice estimated composite GI ~50–58
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K et al. — American Journal of Clinical Nutrition 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for hotplate beef set. GI estimated as composite: steamed white rice (GI ~64, University of Sydney GI Database) contributes ~40% of carbohydrate; beef and vegetables contribute negligible GI. Weighted composite GI estimated at ~54 (low). Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 64.8) / 100 ≈ 35.0, where carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 16.2 × 4 = 64.8 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy-based sauce (~420 mg sodium per 100 g; ~1680 mg per full set serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid soy-based sauces in this dish and request reduced-sodium preparation, as a single serving may contribute up to 73% of the WHO-recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~54) driven by steamed white rice component; moderate carbohydrate load per serving (~65 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be mindful of the white rice portion in this set, as it contributes a low glycemic load; substituting brown rice or reducing rice portion size can help moderate postprandial blood glucose response, despite the overall low GI.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from beef (~1.7 g per 100 g) and elevated sodium from sauce; lean beef cuts and vegetable content provide beneficial protein and micronutrients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, this dish should be consumed in moderation due to its sodium and saturated fat content; choosing leaner beef cuts and requesting low-sodium sauce preparation can reduce cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hotplate Beef Set (铁板牛肉套餐; Japanese: 鉄板ビーフセット) is a teppanyaki-style dish popular across Japanese and pan-Asian restaurant menus in Singapore and Southeast Asia. The dish features thinly sliced or portioned beef cooked on a cast-iron hotplate (teppan) at high heat, producing a characteristic sizzle and caramelised crust. It is typically served with stir-fried or sautéed mixed vegetables such as onion, capsicum, and bean sprouts, and accompanied by steamed white rice, making it a complete set meal. The beef is seasoned or glazed with a savory sauce — commonly soy-based, teriyaki, or black pepper — which contributes significant umami flavour and elevated sodium content. The hotplate retains heat at the table, continuing to cook the food slightly after service, which is a defining sensory feature of the dish. Nutritionally, the set provides a balanced macronutrient profile per serving: approximately 592 kcal, 42 g protein, 19 g fat, and 65 g carbohydrate for a 400 g serving. The beef component supplies high-quality complete protein, haem iron, zinc, vitamin B12, and niacin. The vegetable component contributes dietary fibre, vitamin C, and potassium. The primary clinical concern is sodium: the soy-based sauce can contribute up to 1680 mg sodium per serving, which is significant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The low glycemic index (~54) and moderate glycemic load (~35) are relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management, primarily driven by the white rice component. This dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans, and contains soy and wheat allergens from the sauce. It is commonly found on Japanese restaurant menus, Korean-Japanese fusion menus, and Zi Char-style establishments in Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in teppanyaki beef set?
Is teppan beef set suitable for diabetics?
Teppanyaki beef set GI glycemic index
Can I eat hotplate beef set with high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for hotplate beef set exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data using a weighted composite approach across the four main components of the set (beef, vegetables, sauce, rice). GI is an analogous estimate based on the white rice GI from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is high quality and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component sources used for this composite estimate. 2. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and should be treated as approximate. 3. The cuisine field is set to Japanese per the input record; however, the serving restaurant is listed as Korean Cuisine — Funan Mall, and the cuisine_tags include Japanese, Korean, Ramen, Zi Char, and Seafood, indicating a pan-Asian fusion context. The dish name and preparation style (teppanyaki) are Japanese in origin. 4. Halal status is not confirmed; soy sauce may contain wheat and the beef sourcing is unknown. 5. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (54 × 64.8) / 100 = 35.0, rounded to 35.1 for the 400 g set. 6. No zero-value core macros are present in this record; zero-macro justification rule does not apply. 7. All -1 sentinel values in per_100g represent nutrients not reported in the composite source data; vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries used.
